file_name: Zafra - 2025 - Application of deep learning to detect melanoma on dermoscopy images.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning, specifically the EfficientNet V2 architecture (including variants like efficientnetv2-l-21k-ft1k), to classify dermoscopy images for melanoma detection. The model was trained on 919 images from the HAM10000 dataset to distinguish between melanoma and nonmelanoma lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work where a novel deep learning model (MelanomaENV2) was developed and trained on the HAM10000 dataset. It includes specific methodology sections detailing data preprocessing, model architecture (EfficientNet V2 variants), training parameters (epochs, hardware), and reports original quantitative results (precision, recall, F1 score, AUC-ROC) from testing the model, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Application of Deep Learning to Detect Melanoma on Dermoscopy Images
brief_summary: This study developed MelanomaENV2, a deep learning model based on EfficientNet V2 architectures, to classify dermoscopy images for melanoma detection. The model was trained on a subset of 919 histopathologically confirmed images from the HAM10000 dataset, divided into 400 melanoma and 519 nonmelanoma cases. Eight EfficientNet V2 variants were evaluated using precision, recall, F1 score, accuracy, and AUC-ROC metrics after 30 epochs of training. The efficientnetv2-l-21k-ft1k variant achieved the highest overall performance, with a precision of 0.73, recall of 0.69, F1 score of 0.71, and AUC-ROC of 0.72. The results suggest that while the model shows promise for clinical triage, its performance remains below the threshold for autonomous decision-making, highlighting the need for further improvements.

lesion_types_studied.details: Positive class in the binary classification task; 400 histopathologically confirmed cases used in the training/testing subset of HAM10000.

Negative class in the binary classification task; 519 histopathologically confirmed cases used in the training/testing subset of HAM10000. This group aggregates multiple benign and malignant non-melanoma lesions.

all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the model was developed to 'classify dermoscopy images as melanoma or nonmelanoma' using a 'binary classification task'. The target is a discrete diagnostic category derived from histopathologically confirmed images from the HAM10000 dataset. Performance was evaluated using classification metrics such as precision, recall, F1 score, accuracy, and AUC-ROC.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 (Human Against Machine dataset)
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, sourced from Harvard Dataverse. The study utilized a subset of this dataset containing only histopathologically confirmed images.

use.training: Used for training the MelanomaENV2 model (EfficientNet V2 variants) with an 80% split (736 images).
use.testing: Used for testing the model with a 20% split (183 images).

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics (precision, recall, F1 score, accuracy, AUC-ROC) across different model variants without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.experiment: Binary classification (melanoma vs. nonmelanoma) using EfficientNet V2 variants
HAM10000.notes: The efficientnetv2-l-21k-ft1k variant achieved the highest overall performance with a precision of 0.73, recall of 0.69, F1 score of 0.71, training accuracy of 0.88, test accuracy of 0.72, and AUC-ROC of 0.72. Training was conducted for 30 epochs. The efficientnetv2-xl-21k variant exhibited the lowest precision (0.41). The F1 score ranged from 0.52 to 0.74 across variants.

architecture_used.main_model: EfficientNet V2 (specifically the efficientnetv2-l-21k-ft1k variant was identified as the best performing model for the MelanomaENV2 system)
architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained on ImageNet (21k classes) and fine-tuned on ImageNet (1k classes), indicated by the '-21k-ft1k' suffix in the variant name

architecture_details.activation_function: Sigmoid activation function in the output layer

training_details.hardware: Google Colab free-tier (12.7 GB RAM, 78 GB disk space, 15 GB GPU RAM)

preprocessing_techniques: Image rescaling to standardize input size to 450x450 pixels
conclusions: The efficientnetv2-l-21k-ft1k variant achieved the highest overall performance among the tested EfficientNet V2 models, with a precision of 0.73, recall of 0.69, F1 score of 0.71, test accuracy of 0.72, and AUC-ROC of 0.72.

The model's moderate AUC-ROC of 0.72 indicates a reliable yet improvable level of discriminative ability, suggesting potential utility in clinical triage systems for primary care or community health settings.
The model's current performance remains below the threshold required for autonomous clinical decision-making.

Future improvements could be achieved through ensemble learning, integration of metadata, training with larger and more diverse datasets, incorporation of explainability features (e.g., Grad-CAM), testing in prospective clinical settings, and expansion into multi-class lesion classification.
important_remarks: The study relies on a very small subset of the HAM10000 dataset (919 images) restricted to histopathologically confirmed cases, which limits generalizability and statistical power compared to using the full dataset.
The model was trained and evaluated on a single dataset (HAM10000) without external validation on independent cohorts, raising concerns about dataset bias and overfitting to specific image sources.

Performance metrics are moderate (AUC-ROC 0.72, F1 score 0.71), and the authors explicitly state the model is below the threshold for autonomous clinical decision-making, limiting immediate clinical deployment.
No data augmentation techniques were employed, which may contribute to the limited performance given the small sample size (n=919).

The 'nonmelanoma' class aggregates multiple distinct lesion types (benign keratosis, basal cell carcinoma, etc.), preventing the model from distinguishing between specific benign and malignant non-melanoma pathologies.

Training was conducted on Google Colab free-tier hardware, which may introduce variability in reproducibility and computational constraints compared to dedicated high-performance computing environments.

The paper does not report statistical significance testing (p-values or confidence intervals) for the performance metrics, making it difficult to assess the robustness of the differences between model variants.
